era_context: Alexander III is consolidating his control over Bactria and Sogdiana and preparing for his invasion of India. The Hellenistic world is being reshaped by Alexander's conquests. In India, the Mauryan Empire is at the height of its early expansion. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin rising as a dominant power. In Greece, the city-states remain under Macedonian hegemony.

events.title: Alexander's Marriage to Roxana and Consolidation of Bactrian Rule
events.description: In 327 BCE, Alexander married Roxana (Roxana), the daughter of the Bactrian nobleman Oxyartes. The marriage was a political act designed to consolidate Alexander's rule over Bactria by legitimizing his authority through a union with the local aristocracy. Roxana was reportedly of great beauty, and the marriage was celebrated with a grand wedding feast. The marriage also marked the completion of Alexander's conquest of Bactria and Sogdiana, as the rebellion of Spitamenes had been suppressed by this time. Roxana would become Alexander's primary wife and would bear him a son, Alexander IV, after his death.
events.certainty_note: Alexander's marriage to Roxana is well-attested in multiple Greek sources. The date is placed in 327 BCE by modern scholarship.

events.title: Alexander's Adoption of Persian Customs and Court Intrigues
events.description: In 327 BCE, Alexander increasingly adopted Persian customs and court practices, including the wearing of Persian dress, the practice of proskynesis (ritual obeisance), and the adoption of Persian administrative structures. These changes were controversial among the Macedonian court and military, who viewed them as a betrayal of Greek and Macedonian traditions. The adoption of Persian customs reflected Alexander's vision of a unified Greco-Persian empire and his desire to be accepted as a legitimate successor to the Achaemenid kings. However, the changes also contributed to growing tensions within Alexander's court and military, as some Macedonian nobles resisted what they saw as Alexander's transformation into an Oriental despot.
events.certainty_note: Alexander's adoption of Persian customs is well-attested in multiple Greek sources. The process was gradual, but 327 BCE marks a period of intensified adoption of Persian practices.
cross_references: Follows from 328-002 (Executions of Philotas and Parmenion)
Led to 325-001 (Mutiny at the Hyphasis River)

events.title: Mauryan Empire Reaches Peak of Early Expansion
events.description: By 327 BCE, the Mauryan Empire under Chandragupta had reached the peak of its early expansion, controlling much of northern and central India. The empire had consolidated its administrative structures and military forces, establishing a centralized bureaucracy and a standing army. The Mauryan Empire was now the dominant power in the Indian subcontinent, and Chandragupta was recognized as one of the major rulers of the ancient world. The empire's rapid growth was facilitated by Chandragupta's military genius and Kautilya's political and administrative innovations.
events.certainty_note: The expansion of the Mauryan Empire is attested in later Sanskrit sources. The precise dating of events in 327 BCE is uncertain; Indian chronology for this period relies on later compilations and synchronisms with Greek accounts.

events.title: Qin State Consolidation and Territorial Expansion
events.description: The state of Qin continued its consolidation and territorial expansion under King Huiwen of Qin. Qin pursued aggressive military campaigns against neighboring states, incorporating new territories and expanding its power base. The state's success was based on military innovation, administrative centralization, and the recruitment of talented strategists. By 327 BCE, Qin was consolidating its position as one of the major powers in the Warring States system, though it would not achieve hegemony until the late 3rd century BCE.
key_figures: King Huiwen of Qin (Qin ruler, r. 338–311 BCE)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are compiled in texts written centuries later. The Shiji is the primary source, but its chronology for this era is subject to scholarly revision.
cross_references: Part of broader Warring States competition

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Alexander's marriage to Roxana is debated by modern scholars. Some argue that it was primarily a political act with little romantic significance, while others suggest that Alexander may have had genuine affection for Roxana. The extent to which Alexander's adoption of Persian customs was a deliberate policy versus a gradual cultural assimilation is disputed. Some scholars argue that Alexander's adoption of Persian customs was less radical than the Greek sources suggest, and that he maintained a fundamentally Macedonian identity. The chronology of the Mauryan Empire's expansion is debated; some scholars argue that the consolidation was more gradual than the traditional accounts suggest.
historiographic_note: The year 327 BCE is well-documented for Alexander's campaigns and personal life through Greek and later Greco-Roman sources, though these are not contemporary. The sources on Alexander's adoption of Persian customs reflect Greek perspectives and may be biased against such practices. For India, sources are sparse and compiled centuries later; the chronology is debated. For China, the Shiji provides a framework, but its accuracy for specific years is uncertain. The historical record is heavily skewed toward the Mediterranean, Near East, and parts of Asia; events in sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: The completion of Alexander's conquest of Bactria and Sogdiana, marked by his marriage to Roxana, allowed him to prepare for his invasion of India
Alexander's adoption of Persian customs and increasing paranoia contributed to tensions that would lead to the mutiny at the Hyphasis River in 325 BCE
The Mauryan Empire's expansion eventually led to conflict with Seleucus I, Alexander's successor in the Near East
